story.headline: US concludes 11th round of strikes on Iran as Houthis blockade Bab al-Mandeb; Hegseth seeks $87.6bn more
story.summary: The US-Israel war on Iran widened as Yemen's Houthis closed the Bab al-Mandeb strait and Hegseth told senators the war has cost the US $37.5bn.
story.display_body: The US-Israel war on Iran widened into the Red Sea on Tuesday 21 July as Yemen's Houthis declared a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia, closing the Bab al-Mandeb strait. Two oil tankers carrying Saudi crude toward Asia turned back after the announcement, compounding disruption already hitting the Strait of Hormuz - the two chokepoints together carry a large share of seaborne oil. The US said it had concluded its 11th round of strikes on Iran and Tehran retaliated; Washington is reportedly weighing an attack on Iran's Pickaxe Mountain, believed to be an underground uranium-enrichment site. Testifying to senators on Wednesday 22 July, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth put the war's cost to the US at $37.5bn so far and requested $87.6bn more, while Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the US remains open to negotiations but that Iran is not serious.
story.why: A second closed chokepoint turns a regional war into a direct shock to global energy shipping, and the funding request signals Washington is bracing for a long campaign, not a quick strike.

story.headline: Zelensky dismisses armed-forces chief Syrsky amid protests, names Drapaty
story.summary: Ukraine's president fired commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrsky on Tuesday and replaced him with Mykhailo Drapaty after protests.
story.display_body: President Volodymyr Zelensky dismissed the commander-in-chief of Ukraine's armed forces, Oleksandr Syrsky, on Tuesday 21 July, and named the popular joint-forces commander Mykhailo Drapaty to replace him. The move follows the 15 July sacking of young defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov - the architect of Ukraine's drone force - which set off mass protests in Kyiv; demonstrators had accused the military leadership of wasting lives. Le Temps frames the shake-up as Zelensky sidelining modernisers in favour of the old guard.
story.why: Replacing both the defence minister and the top general inside a week, under public pressure, points to a real doctrinal fight over how Ukraine fights the war - and rare open dissent at home.

story.headline: US to sign deal giving Saudi Arabia a civil uranium-enrichment program
story.summary: The Trump administration is expected to announce a deal for US firms to build a uranium enrichment complex in Saudi Arabia.
story.display_body: The Trump administration is expected to announce Wednesday 22 July a deal under which US firms would build a uranium-enrichment complex in Saudi Arabia, giving the kingdom a civil nuclear programme. Officials stress the programme is meant for civilian use; critics warn that endorsing enrichment on Saudi soil - as the US is actively bombing Iran to stop its own enrichment - could accelerate a regional arms race.
story.why: Enrichment is the dual-use step that separates a power reactor from a bomb, so a US-blessed Saudi enrichment plant reshapes the Gulf's proliferation math at the worst possible moment.

story.headline: France adopts EU-first ban on social media for under-15s
story.summary: The French Parliament definitively adopted a Macron-backed law barring under-15s from social media, effective September.
story.display_body: The French Parliament definitively adopted on Tuesday 21 July a law barring under-15s from social media - a European first - with the ban set to take effect in September. The Macron-backed text requires platforms to verify users' ages but sets no penalty for families, a gap that drew criticism as unworkable. This finalises the bill the two chambers were only scheduled to vote on a day earlier.
story.why: It is the first hard national age floor for social media in the EU, and a live test of whether platform-side age verification can be enforced at all - with implications for how neighbouring states, including Switzerland, regulate minors online.

story.headline: Trump's 100% generic-drug tariff puts Swiss maker Sandoz on front line
story.summary: Trump announced 100% tariffs on imported generic drugs from 2028, exposing Sandoz whose production is largely in Europe.
story.display_body: Donald Trump's threatened 100% tariff on imported generic drugs, announced Tuesday 21 July and slated to start in 2028, lands squarely on Swiss-based Sandoz. The world's leading generics maker manufactures largely in Europe, so a US import levy - which Washington says is meant to relocate production stateside and which could rise to 200% in 2029 - would hit its US business hard.
story.why: Generics are a thin-margin, high-volume business, and a tariff of this size is less a price signal than a demand to rebuild supply chains, a direct threat to a major Swiss pharma employer.

story.headline: Geneva court suspends canton's pool ban on long swimwear and burkini
story.summary: Geneva justice granted suspensive effect to an appeal against the late-May law banning long clothing in public pools.
story.display_body: Geneva's justice system has suspended the canton's contested law banning long swimwear - including the burkini - in public pools, granting suspensive effect to an appeal. The law took force at the end of May but is now blocked pending the case; it is also being challenged by five communes, among them the city of Geneva itself.
story.why: The suspension is the first serious legal setback for one of Switzerland's most divisive recent cantonal measures, and pits the canton against its own largest city.

story.headline: Skyguide facing structural deficit; transport politicians seek new revenue
story.summary: Swiss air-navigation firm Skyguide faces a CHF40m annual structural deficit from 2030; politicians propose charging foreign users or passengers.
story.display_body: Swiss air-navigation provider Skyguide faces a structural deficit of about CHF 40 million a year from 2030, and transport politicians are pushing for new revenue sources. Skyguide lives mainly on fees airlines pay for landings and overflights, with the Confederation - its main shareholder - already contributing over CHF 120 million for special tasks such as military air control and services in cross-border airspace; that no longer covers costs. Ideas floated include recovering fees from foreign users of Swiss-controlled airspace or charging passengers directly, alongside a federal cost-cutting programme.
story.why: The funding gap forces a choice between higher travel costs and cuts at an operator that has had high-profile outages, touching both flight safety and ticket prices.

story.headline: Swiss drownings rise; at least 15 deaths in a month
story.summary: The Swiss Lifesaving Society says bathing deaths are up year-on-year, with at least 15 drownings in a month.
story.display_body: At least 15 people have drowned in Switzerland in the past month, and the Swiss Lifesaving Society reports bathing deaths running above last year's pace. Two of the recent deaths were in the river Aare; men account for a markedly higher share of cases than women, per SRF. The running toll is still below the exceptional 2022 and 2023 summers.

fields.display_body: At least 15 people have drowned in Switzerland in the past month, and the Swiss Lifesaving Society reports bathing deaths running above last year's pace. Two of the recent deaths were in the river Aare; men account for a markedly higher share of cases than women, per SRF. The running toll is still below the exceptional 2022 and 2023 summers.

story.headline: Heat flows in quantized steps through graphene's fractal quantum-Hall states
story.summary: Johnson-noise thermometry shows thermal conductance is quantized in units of the thermal conductance quantum, tracking Chern numbers, in a bilayer-graphene moire superlattice's Hofstadter states.
story.display_body: In the quantum Hall effect - the regime where a cold two-dimensional electron sheet in a strong magnetic field carries current losslessly along its edges - electrical conduction comes in exact, universal quantized steps. Whether heat is carried the same universal way in the richer Hofstadter states (the fractal energy spectrum electrons form when a magnetic field and a periodic moire lattice act on them together) had never been tested. Using Johnson-noise thermometry - reading temperature off the faint electrical noise that any warm conductor emits - the team measured the thermal conductance of quantum-Hall, Chern-insulator, and interaction-driven symmetry-broken states in a bilayer-graphene/boron-nitride superlattice (moire wavelength about 14 nm), and found it quantized in units of the thermal conductance quantum, tracking each state's Chern number (the integer that labels its topology).
story.why: It shows these exotic topological states move heat as cleanly and universally as they move charge, confirming their fundamental nature and giving a probe of edge modes that ordinary electrical measurements can miss.

Using Johnson-noise thermometry - reading temperature off the faint electrical noise that any warm conductor emits - the team measured the thermal conductance of quantum-Hall, Chern-insulator, and interaction-driven symmetry-broken states in a bilayer-graphene/boron-nitride superlattice (moire wavelength about 14 nm), and found it quantized in units of the thermal conductance quantum, tracking each state's Chern number (the integer that labels its topology).

story.headline: Earth's inner core may be more than 1,000 C cooler than the textbook value
story.summary: Science reports diamond-anvil measurements putting iron's melting point at inner-core-boundary pressure near 4,420-5,220 K, over 1,000 C below the standard estimate, easing the geodynamo puzzle.
story.display_body: Science reports new high-pressure laboratory measurements putting the melting point of iron at inner-core-boundary conditions at roughly 4,420-5,220 K - more than 1,000 C below the ~5,500 C usually quoted - implying a cooler core than the standard picture. The team squeezed iron between diamond anvils to near two million atmospheres (comparable to the outer core) and, instead of laser-heating it, drove it with microsecond electrical pulses for tighter temperature control. A cooler core is more permissive of a thermally driven geodynamo (the churning liquid-iron engine that generates Earth's magnetic field): per the report, core cooling alone could have powered the field for most of Earth's history, until the solid inner core began to freeze a little over half a billion years ago. I could not reach the primary paper or the article's full text from the sandbox, so this rests on Science's account via search snippet - treat the exact figures as provisional pending the paper.
story.why: The core's temperature sets the energy budget for Earth's magnetic shield and the age of the solid inner core; shaving 1,000 C off it reshuffles both.

story.headline: A single waxy molecule, not just bullying, keeps naked mole-rat queens in charge
story.summary: Nature study identifies isopropyl myristate, an ester enriched in queens, as the olfactory signal that suppresses reproduction in subordinate naked mole-rats.
story.display_body: Naked mole-rats are one of the very few eusocial mammals: a single queen breeds while every other female stays infertile, and how the queen enforces that had been unclear. This study names the signal - isopropyl myristate, a low-volatility ester (a waxy, barely-evaporating oily molecule) strongly enriched in queens and nearly absent from non-breeders. Olfactory neurons in the nose and brain detect it; exposure shifts the hormones prolactin and progesterone in subordinates to suppress their reproduction and provokes avoidance in high-ranking rivals. In behavioural tests, adding the ester daily to a queenless colony prevented a new queen from emerging, while withdrawing it triggered aggression and reproductive competition. The same molecule showed up in breeding females of related Fukomys mole-rats, hinting the mechanism is shared across the group.
story.why: It gives a concrete molecular handle - a chemical the animals can smell - on how a mammalian society centrally controls who gets to reproduce, rather than chalking it up to social stress alone.
affiliations: Max Planck Institute for Brain Research

story.headline: The largest genetic study of borderline personality disorder finds its first solid signals
story.summary: A GWAS of 12,339 cases identifies 11 genomic loci and 9 risk genes for borderline personality disorder, with 17.3% SNP heritability - the first robust common-variant signals.
story.display_body: Borderline personality disorder (BPD) has been understudied genetically and often framed as purely a product of environment. This genome-wide association study - a scan of the whole genome for common DNA variants statistically linked to a trait - pooled 12,339 cases against about 1.04 million controls, with a separate replication sample, and identified 11 independent genomic loci and 9 risk genes. It reports a SNP-based heritability of 17.3% (the share of variation these common variants can account for) and polygenic scores that predict about 4.6% of the risk - a real biological signal but far from an individual-level test. All participants were of European ancestry, a genuine limitation on how broadly the results generalise, and the signals overlap with those of other psychiatric and some physical conditions.
story.why: It is the first robust evidence of a common-variant genetic contribution to a heavily stigmatised condition, supporting a biological component and shared architecture with other disorders - without implying any near-term diagnostic use.

story.headline: Beta Pictoris d: the faintest exoplanet ever directly imaged from the ground
story.summary: ESO's VLT, with an independent JWST detection, directly images a third planet in the Beta Pictoris system - a ~2.4 Jupiter-mass gas giant and the faintest exoplanet yet imaged from Earth.
story.display_body: Astronomers have directly imaged a third planet around Beta Pictoris, a young star 63 light-years away whose disk and planets are a benchmark for how planetary systems form. Direct imaging - photographing a planet's own light right next to its far brighter host - normally only works for bright, massive planets, so Beta Pictoris d is a milestone: it is about 100 times fainter than the famous Beta Pic b and, corrected for distance, the faintest exoplanet yet imaged from Earth. It is a gas giant of roughly 2.4 Jupiter masses (against ~10 for b and c) on a much wider orbit. The team spotted it with the Very Large Telescope's ERIS instrument, then found it lurking in archival VLT/SPHERE images going back 11 years; an independent group led by Aidan Gibbs (University of California) detected the same planet with JWST.
story.why: Beta Pictoris becomes only the second system, after HR 8799, with more than two directly imaged planets - a natural laboratory for comparing worlds born from the same disk, and a taste of what ESO's Extremely Large Telescope should routinely reach.

story.headline: One JWST instrument mode, two atmosphere fingerprints for a hot Jupiter
story.summary: A phase-resolved cross-correlation technique extracts WASP-39 b's atmospheric metallicity and C/O ratio from a single archival JWST NIRSpec mode, instead of the usual multi-mode campaign.
story.display_body: Reading an exoplanet atmosphere's metallicity (how enriched it is in elements heavier than hydrogen and helium) and its carbon-to-oxygen ratio - both clues to where in its disk the planet formed - usually demands several JWST instrument modes stitched across ~1-5 microns. This work shows both can be pulled from a single archival transit of the well-studied hot Jupiter WASP-39 b, taken with one NIRSpec mode, by importing a technique from ground-based astronomy: phase-resolved cross-correlation at the detector's native pixel resolution, which matches the full-resolution spectrum against molecular templates instead of collapsing it into a coarse light curve.
story.why: If one instrument mode can do the job, JWST can characterise more planet atmospheres per hour of scarce telescope time - a practical multiplier for the field.
